file_name: LECLANCHÉ S.A. - JOINT DEVELOPMENT AND MARKETING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with Swiss law. The conflict of law rules and the United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods do not apply. The Parties irrevocably agree that the courts of the city of Zurich have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text:
This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with Swiss law. The conflict of law rules and the United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods do not apply. The Parties irrevocably agree that the courts of the city of Zurich have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement.

Oak Ridge shall arrange product liability and warranty insurance.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the responsibility for Oak Ridge to arrange product liability and warranty insurance to the governing law section is misleading and obscures its operational context.
perturbation.contradicted_law: United States law regarding clear and conspicuous disclosure of warranty and liability terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the responsibility for Oak Ridge to secure product liability and warranty insurance to the governing law section buries a key operational term within a legal clause, failing to prominently disclose this obligation as required by U.S. warranty law, especially the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act, which emphasizes clear and conspicuous disclosure of warranty terms and obligations.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 50 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC Ch. 50 : CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 50 —CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES Sec. 2301. Definitions. 2302. Rules governing contents of warranties. 2303. Designation of written warranties. 2304. Federal minimum standards for warranties. 2305. Full and limited warranting of a consumer product. 2306. Service contracts; rules for full, clear and conspicuous disclosure of terms and conditions; addition to or in lieu of written warranty. 2307. Designation of representatives by warrantor to perform duties under written or implied warranty. 2308. Implied warranties. 2309. Procedures applicable to promulgation of rules by Commission. 2310. Remedies in consumer disputes. 2311. Applicability to other laws. 2312. Effective dates. §2301. Definitions For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term "consumer product" means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission. (3) The term "consumer" means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product, any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract). (4) The term "supplier" means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term "warrantor" means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty. (6) The term "written warranty" means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a consumer product by a s
perturbation.justification: The changed text moves the responsibility for Oak Ridge to arrange product liability and warranty insurance into the governing law section. This constitutes a structural flaw, as it buries a key operational term within a legal clause. This contradicts the need for clear and conspicuous disclosure of warranty terms, potentially violating consumer warranty laws, particularly those emphasizing transparency, such as the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 15 USC Ch. 50, concerns consumer product warranties and mentions the need for rules governing the contents of warranties and service contracts, underscoring the legal relevance of clearly disclosing warranty obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall keep strictly secret and confidential any and all confidential information relating to each other's business and/or to the contents of this Agreement and shall not, in any manner whatsoever, disclose or permit any of its agents, representatives, employees, attorneys, accountants or advisors to disclose any confidential information to any person or entity whatsoever without the prior written consent of the other Party. The Parties shall take all necessary steps to safeguard the secrecy and confidentiality of all confidential information and all materials and to ensure that such confidential information and material obtained in connection with this Agreement is disclosed only to authorized persons who need to know such information for the purpose of performing their duties on behalf of the company.
perturbation.changed_text:
The Parties shall keep strictly secret and confidential any and all confidential information relating to each other's business and/or to the contents of this Agreement and shall not, in any manner whatsoever, disclose or permit any of its agents, representatives, employees, attorneys, accountants or advisors to disclose any confidential information to any person or entity whatsoever without the prior written consent of the other Party. The Parties shall take all necessary steps to safeguard the secrecy and confidentiality of all confidential information and all materials and to ensure that such confidential information and material obtained in connection with this Agreement is disclosed only to authorized persons who need to know such information for the purpose of performing their duties on behalf of the company.

Oak Ridge shall arrange product liability and warranty insurance.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the responsibility for Oak Ridge to arrange product liability and warranty insurance into the confidentiality section obscures its true meaning and enforceability by associating it with privacy obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: United States law regarding clear and conspicuous disclosure of warranty and liability terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: Embedding the term about Oak Ridge's insurance responsibility within the confidentiality clause violates the principle of clear and conspicuous disclosure as mandated by U.S. warranty laws like the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act. This placement makes it difficult for a reader to readily identify and understand the responsibilities related to product liability and warranty insurance.

perturbation.justification: The altered text places the obligation for Oak Ridge to arrange product liability and warranty insurance within a confidentiality clause. The Law Explanation suggests this violates the principle of clear disclosure as mandated by warranty laws. The scraped law snippet 2 refers to Title 15, Chapter 50, which pertains to consumer product warranties, including clear disclosure of terms. Placing a warranty obligation within a confidentiality clause obscures the meaning and enforceability of the insurance term. This structural flaw results in a contradiction because the warranty information is not presented in a clear and conspicuous manner, making it difficult for parties to identify and understand these obligations. The Perturbation Explanation supports this view, suggesting the placement is misleading.

perturbation.explanation: Moving the responsibility for Oak Ridge to arrange product liability and warranty insurance to the Term and Termination section obscures its meaning and enforceability by associating it with temporal validity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: United States law regarding clear and conspicuous disclosure of warranty and liability terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: Including the term regarding Oak Ridge's duty to obtain product liability and warranty insurance within the termination clause makes it harder to locate and understand, thereby reducing its enforceability and contravening the clear and conspicuous requirement mandated by U.S. warranty law.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation moves the responsibility for Oak Ridge to arrange product liability and warranty insurance to the Term and Termination section. This makes the obligation harder to locate and understand, which, according to the Law Explanation, reduces its enforceability and contravenes the clear and conspicuous requirement mandated by U.S. warranty law. While the provided snippets do not explicitly state the 'clear and conspicuous requirement,' snippet 2 references Title 15, Chapter 50, which pertains to consumer product warranties. The Law Explanation and the type of perturbation suggest a violation of warranty law due to the structural flaw impacting clarity and enforceability. Therefore, this qualifies as a Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction. The contradiction exists because a requirement for insurance is obscured by being placed within a termination clause, thereby potentially reducing enforceability of the warranty.
